content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 31, 1990

Richard Woolstrum
18 South Main Street
Lodi, CA 95240

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-188

Dear Mr. Woolstrum:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act of 1974.¹ You plan to be a candidate
for county supervisor in San Joaquin County. All of the
conclusions in this letter are based on the assumption that you
are a candidate for elective office.

QUESTION

1. Are "public commentary ads" placed in a local newspaper,
which list your name or display your picture, and are paid for by
Glory Properties, Inc., a corporation owned exclusively by you,
contributions to your campaign that are subject to the
contribution limitations?

2. Will the advertisements need to be identified by the name
of your committee and its campaign identification number?

3. If paying for these advertisements is considered a
contribution to your campaign, how much may Glory Properties, Inc.
spend after you declare your candidacy?

4. Since Glory Properties, Inc. owes you more than $100,000,
could Glory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for the
advertisements over any applicable contribution limits and deduct
the excess from the amount owed to you?

5. If the scenario in question #4 above is not allowed, may
you or your campaign pay for the advertisements as long as "paid
for by..." and your committee's identification number appear on
the advertisements?

_____________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Richard Woolstrum
Page Two

6. If you or your campaign pay for the advertisements, may
their format change to include political commentary in support of
or opposition to candidates?

7. Must your campaign committee report, as in-kind
contributions, payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. for
advertisements which appeared in the newspapers before you
declared your candidacy?

8. If your name and photograph were removed from the
advertisements, could Glory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for
the advertisements without the payments being considered in-kind
contributions, as long as no opposition or support of candidates
or ballot measures are included in the commentary?

9. May Glory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for the ads
without the payments being considered in-kind contributions if
their format does not change and your wife, Joani Woolstrum, is
listed as author?

10. Are there limits on the money or services an individual
or corporation may loan a candidate's campaign?

11. May a candidate's committee transfer its debt obligation
to another committee controlled by the same candidate?

12. May candidates continue to raise money after their
election to pay outstanding debts?

13. Where must the name and campaign identification number
of the committee appear on brochures, envelopes, postcards, or
business cards?

14. Must the candidate's voice be a part of any radio or
television advertisement?

15. May a non-profit organization you and your wife direct
place advertisements seeking donations for the non-profit
organization, listing your name as a contact person without the
cost of the advertisements being considered in-kind contributions
to your campaign?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Newspaper advertisements paid for by your corporation
which list your name or display your picture are in-kind
contributions to your campaign and are subject to the limitations.
Corporate funds are not the personal funds of a candidate, even if
the corporation is wholly-owned by the candidate. Since a
corporation's limitation is $1,000 per fiscal year (July 1 through

Richard Woolstrum
Page Three

June 30) per candidate and includes in-kind contributions, Glory
Properties, Inc. may not pay for more than $1,000 worth of
advertising in a fiscal year. In addition, Glory Properties, Inc.
must cumulate its payments for the advertisements with any other
contributions it makes to your campaign to determine if the $1,000
limit has been reached.

However, since contributions you make to your own campaign
are not subject to the contribution limitations, you may pay for
these advertisements out of your own personal funds without
consideration of the limits.

2. The Political Reform Act does not require that you or
your committee be identified either by name or identification
number in newspaper advertisements.

3. After you become a candidate for elective office, Glory
Properties, Inc. may contribute up to $1,000 each fiscal year to
your candidacy. If Glory Properties, Inc. makes no other
contributions to your campaign within a fiscal year, it may pay
for up to $1,000 in newspaper advertisements to support your
candidacy.

4. Glory Properties, Inc. may not pay directly for the
newspaper advertisements you mention as repayment of monies owed
to you if the amount Glory Properties, Inc. pays, cumulated with
all other contributions it makes to your campaign, exceeds $1,000
in a fiscal year.

5. Your campaign may continue to pay for the advertisements
you mention. If you choose to pay for the advertisements out of
personal funds, you must first place your funds into the campaign
bank account prior to expenditure. No identification of your
committee or its identification number is required on any
newspaper advertisements.

6. You may use personal funds to pay for newspaper
advertisements which support or oppose OTHER candidates for
elective office. If you place these advertisements for other
candidates at the behest of the other candidates, you may not
spend more than $1,000 in the aggregate in a fiscal year per
candidate. If, however, you use personal funds to place
advertisements supporting or opposing other candidates and do not
make the payments at the behest of the candidates, there are no
limits on how much you may spend. You must report these payments
on the Allocation Page of the Form 490. In addition, please see
page 39 of the enclosed Information Manual A concerning the
Requirement to file Supplemental Independent Expenditure Reports
(Form 465).

Richard Woolstrum
Page Four

     If you want to use personal funds to pay for advertisements
which support your own candidacy, you must deposit the funds into
your campaign bank account prior to expenditure.  There are no
limits on the amount of your personal funds you may use to support
your own election.

     7.  If Glory Properties, Inc. pays for a newspaper
advertisement which includes your name or photograph with a view
to bringing about your nomination or election to any elective
office, it has made a contribution to you.  In such a case, you
are required to report and count toward the contribution limit of
$1,000 any payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. for the
newspaper advertisements.

     8.  As long as you, your campaign, or your opponent(s) are
not identified in the advertisements, any payments made by Glory
Properties, Inc. for the advertisements would not be considered
contributions and, thus, would not be subject to the contribution
limitations.

     9.  Your wife may be listed as author of the advertisements
without the advertisements being considered contributions to your
campaign.

     10.  Under the Political Reform Act, loans for political
purposes are contributions.  Thus, an individual (other than the
candidate) or corporation must cumulate loans made to a candidate
with any other contributions the individual or corporation has made
to the same candidate during a fiscal year to determine if the
contribution limit has been reached for that particular candidate.

     Personal services are not contributions, unless the person
performing the services is paid by a third party.  In this case,
if an individual pays another person for services rendered to the
campaign in anticipation of being repaid by the campaign, the
individual has made an in-kind loan to the campaign and must
cumulate the amount paid with any other contributions the
individual has made to the candidate within a fiscal year to
determine if the limits have been reached.

     11.  Debt obligation is not transferable between a
candidate's own committees.  However, contributions received are
transferable between a candidate's controlled committees.  Thus,
funds may be transferred between one controlled committee and
another committee controlled by the same candidate to retire the
debt of the first committee.

     12.  Candidate-controlled committees may continue to raise
money after an election to pay any outstanding debts, subject to
the fiscal year contribution limitations.

Richard Woolstrum
Page Five

13. The name and address of the committee as listed with the
Secretary of State are required to be listed in no less than 6-
point type on the outside of any mass mailing (more than 200
pieces of similar or identical pieces of mail sent in a calendar
month).

There are no identification requirements for business cards,
postcards, brochures, or envelopes, unless they meet the above
definition of a mass mailing.

14. There is no requirement under the Act that a candidate's
voice be part of any radio or television advertisement supporting
his or her candidacy. Please contact the Federal Communications
Commission for advice concerning the regulation of radio and
television advertisements under federal law.

15. Under the facts presented, payments made by a non-profit
organization for newspaper advertisements containing your name or
photograph are considered in-kind contributions from the non-
profit organization to your campaign and must be cumulated with
any other contributions the non-profit organization makes to your
campaign within the same fiscal year to determine if the
contribution limits have been reached. Under the Political Reform
Act, non-profit organizations making political contributions for
the first time are limited to $1,000 per candidate per fiscal
year. Please contact the Internal Revenue Service at (800) 424-
1040 for any restrictions that may apply to non-profit
organizations making political contributions.

FACTS

You intend to be a candidate for county supervisor in San
Joaquin County.

You own 100% of the stock in Glory Properties, Inc. This
corporation owns the Golden Era Hotel. Glory Properties places
"public commentaries" in a local newspaper for business purposes
under your name and that of Golden Era Hotel.

ANALYSIS

"Contribution" is defined by Section 82015, in part, as "a
payment....except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. An
expenditure made at the behest of a candidate... is a contribution
to the candidate... unless full and adequate consideration is

Richard Woolstrum
Page Six


received for making the expenditure."2  Regulation 18215 defines
political purpose as "influencing or attempting to influence the
voters for or against the ... election of a candidate...."
Payments made at your behest are contributions.

     We have previously advised that advertisements produced by a
non-profit university to promote its professional studies program
in which the name, title, and photograph of a candidate for
elective office appear, even though no advocacy is made, are
contributions to the candidate.  (McCarthy Advice Letter,
No. A-90-163, copy enclosed.)

     Since the payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. are
controlled by you, its payments for newspaper advertisements in
which you are identified are made at your behest.  (Regulation
18215.)  Although one of the factors considered in the McCarthy
advice letter was the advertisement's proximity to the candidate's
election, the fact that the newspaper advertisements placed by
Glory Properties, Inc. express your political views supports the
conclusion that payments made at your behest for the
advertisements are contributions to your campaign.

     Furthermore, Section 82007 defines candidate, in pertinent
part, as:

     ...an individual who...gives his or her consent for
     any other person to...make an expenditure with a view
     to bringing about his or her nomination or election to
     elective office, whether or not the specific elective
     office which he or she will seek, is known at the time
     the expenditure is made and whether or not he or she
     has announced his or her candidacy or filed a
     declaration of candidacy at such time.

     Therefore, payments by Glory Properties, Inc. for
advertisements of the type described above will be contributions
to you if they were made with a view to bringing about your
nomination or election to office regardless of whether you have
announced your candidacy or filed a declaration of candidacy.


_____________________________

2    "Made at the behest" means, in part, "a payment made under
the control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of
a candidate, controlled committee, official committee of a
political party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes."  (Regulation 18215(b).)

Richard Woolstrum
Page Seven

The Act provides for an exception to the contribution
limitations for the personal funds of a candidate making
contributions to his or her own campaign. (Section 85301(b).)
However, funds of a wholly-owned corporation are not considered to
be the personal funds of the candidate.

A corporation may not contribute more than $1,000 in a fiscal
year to any one candidate. (Section 85301.) Section 85102
includes a corporation in the definition of "person."

Section 85201 requires any personal funds of a candidate to
be deposited into the campaign bank account prior to being
expended for campaign purposes.

There is no prohibition against a candidate using personal
funds to support or oppose other candidates. Of course, the
candidate is subject to the contribution limits as any other
person under the law. However, if payments are made out of
personal funds to expressly advocate the nomination, election, or
defeat of any other candidate, and the payments are not made at
the behest of the affected candidate, these payments are
"independent expenditures" and are not subject to the contribution
limits. (Section 82031.)

Section 85304 prohibits transfers of any contribution from
one candidate to another. Thus, you may not use your campaign
funds to support another candidate. The trust provisions of
Section 85202 states:

All contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust
for expenses associated with the election of
the candidate...

This provision prohibits you from using campaign funds to make
contributions or independent expenditures to support or oppose
other candidates for state or local elective office.

Section 84211(j)(5) requires that contributions of $100 or
more made by the candidate from personal funds in a calendar year
in support or opposition to other candidates or ballot measures be
itemized. On the Form 490, these contributions must be reported
on the Allocation Page.

Section 82015 specifies that "volunteer personal services"
are not contributions.

Richard Woolstrum
Page Eight

Even though Section 85202 prohibits a candidate from
transferring campaign contributions received for one office to the
same candidate's campaign committee for another office, an order
of the United States District Court, Eastern District of
California enjoins the Commission from enforcing this provision.
Therefore, contributions received by one committee controlled by a
candidate may be transferred to another committee controlled by
the same candidate. (SEIU, AFL-CIO, et al. v. FPPC, Case No. CIVS
89-0433 LKK-JFM.)

Section 84305 provides that

...no candidate or committee shall send a mass mailing unless
the name, street address, and city of the candidate or
committee are shown on the outside of each piece of mail in
the mass mailing....

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabjin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

cc: Dennis Morita, CPA

If you want to use personal funds to pay for advertisements

7. If Glory Properties, Inc. pays for a nelrspaper
advertisernent rrhich includes your name or photograph with a view
to bringing about your nomination or election to any elective
office, it has made a contribution to you. In such a case, you
are required to report and count tolrard the contribution limit of
$1,ooo any payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. for the
nehrspaper advertisements.
8. As long as you, your campaign, or your opponent(s) are
not identified in the advertisenents, any payments made by clory
Properties, Inc. for the advertisements lrould not be cons j.dered
contributions and, thus, would not be subject to the contribution
l initations .
9. Your wife may be listed as author of the advertisements
without the advertisenents being considered contributions to your
carnpaign.
10. Under the Political Reform Act, Ioans for poJ-itical
purposes are contributions. 
Thus, an individual (other than the
candidate) or corporation must cumulate loans made to a candidate
with any other contribution the individual or corporation has made
to the sane candidate during a fiscal year to deternine if the
contribution limit has been reached for that particuJ.ar candidate.
Personal services are not contributions, unless the person
perforrning the services is paid by a third party. 
In this case,
if an individual pays another person for services rendered to the
canpaign in anticipation of being repaid by the campaign, the
individual has made an in-kind loan to the campaign and must
cumulate the anount paid with any other contributions the
individual has made to the candidate within a fiscal year to
determine if the lirnits have been reached.
11. Debt obligation is not transferable between a
candidaters olrn conmittees. However, contributions received are
transferable between a candidate,s controlled committees. Thus,
funds may be transferred between one controlled committee anal
another committee controrled bv the same candi.date to retire the
debt of the first 
cornrnittee.
t2. 
Candidate-control led committees may continue to raise
money after an election to pay.any outstandiig debts, subject to
the fiscal year contribution lirnitations.


Richard Woolstrum
Page Five

secretary of State are required to be l-isted in no less than G-
point type on the outside of any mass naiLing (more than 2OO
pieces of sinilar or identical pieces of mail sent in a calendar
ruonth).
There are no identification 
requirernents for business cards,
postcards, brochures, or envelopes, unless they meet the above
definition of a mass nailing.
14. There is no requirenent under the Act that a candidate,s
voice be part of any radio or television advertisement supporting
his or her candidacy. Please contact the Federal Comnunications
Conmission for advice concerning the regulation of radio and
television advertisements under federal Iaw.
15. Under the facts presented, payrnents made by a non-profit
organization for newspaper advertisements containing your name or
photograph are considered in-kind contributions from the non-
profit orgranization to your campaign and must be curnulated with
any other contributions the non-profit organization makes to your
campaign within the same fiscal year to determine if the
contribution limits have been reached. Under the Political 
Reform
Act, non-profit organizations naking politicaJ. contributions for
the first time are linited to $1,000 per candidate per fiscal
year. Please contact the Internal Revenue Service at (8O0) 424-
1040 for any restrictions that nay apply to non-profit
organizations making political contributions.

Joaquin County.

I'public comnentariesrr in a local newspaper for business purposes

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission May 31, 1990 Richard Woolstrum 18 South Main Street Lodi, CA 95240 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-90-188 Dear Mr. Woolstrum: You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.¹ You plan to be a candidate for county supervisor in San Joaquin County. All of the conclusions in this letter are based on the assumption that you are a candidate for elective office. QUESTION 1. Are "public commentary ads" placed in a local newspaper, which list your name or display your picture, and are paid for by Glory Properties, Inc., a corporation owned exclusively by you, contributions to your campaign that are subject to the contribution limitations? 2. Will the advertisements need to be identified by the name of your committee and its campaign identification number? 3. If paying for these advertisements is considered a contribution to your campaign, how much may Glory Properties, Inc. spend after you declare your candidacy? 4. Since Glory Properties, Inc. owes you more than $100,000, could Glory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for the advertisements over any applicable contribution limits and deduct the excess from the amount owed to you? 5. If the scenario in question #4 above is not allowed, may you or your campaign pay for the advertisements as long as "paid for by..." and your committee's identification number appear on the advertisements? _____________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660 Richard Woolstrum Page Two 6. If you or your campaign pay for the advertisements, may their format change to include political commentary in support of or opposition to candidates? 7. Must your campaign committee report, as in-kind contributions, payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. for advertisements which appeared in the newspapers before you declared your candidacy? 8. If your name and photograph were removed from the advertisements, could Glory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for the advertisements without the payments being considered in-kind contributions, as long as no opposition or support of candidates or ballot measures are included in the commentary? 9. May Glory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for the ads without the payments being considered in-kind contributions if their format does not change and your wife, Joani Woolstrum, is listed as author? 10. Are there limits on the money or services an individual or corporation may loan a candidate's campaign? 11. May a candidate's committee transfer its debt obligation to another committee controlled by the same candidate? 12. May candidates continue to raise money after their election to pay outstanding debts? 13. Where must the name and campaign identification number of the committee appear on brochures, envelopes, postcards, or business cards?
